# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile for a Node.js application that needs various system utilities. Install Node.js, build tools, and other dependencies using apt-get. Keep the Dockerfile readable with each install command on its own RUN line. This is for our development and production environments.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official node runtime as a parent image  #https://hub.docker.com/_/node which provides Node.js & NPM already installed (LTS) version, or any other based on your requirement such latest LTS and greatest(Current), then install the necessary software using 'RUN' command in Dockerfile
FROM node:lts-stretch  #replace with required versions if needed like "alpine" as base image which is lighter weight than Node.js images (consider it stable) . Also you can use a specific tag based on your requirement, for example latest , or any other version number such v12
# Copy the current directory contents into docker image  #This will copy files from local system to Docker Image during Build process - This is good practice. The source in this command should match with what we want build and that folder exists on your host machine, which you can also specify directly if required (optional) e.g., /src
SHELL [ "/bin/bash" ]   #This will allow us to use bash commands inside the Docker image after FROM is runned using 'RUN' command  in dockerfile as a replacement for apt-get, etc . The default shell was defined by your host machine system. After this line of code you can just write what we want from install and also build
COPY ./ /usr/src/app    #This will copy files into the Docker image under '/var/www/html'  on running node app, change as per need (optional) e .g., 'api', './bin/*'. If you don’t specify a path then current directory contents would be copied.
WORKDIR /usr/src/app   #This sets the working directory in docker image to that location where your command line runs inside Docker container — changes with each run of app so needs '/var/www' folder structure if not present already (optional) e .g., 'api'. If it’s absent then /usr/src is set as default.
# Install the dependencies using npm install --production in docker image for building and testing purpose — This will be available inside your container, changes with each run of app so needs installing node_modules folder structure (optional) e .g., 'api'. If it’s absent then /usr/src is set as default.
RUN mkdir -p ./node_modules && chmod +x package.json  #This will make sure the directory for storing npm packages exists and allows scripts to be executed in Dockerfile (optional) e .g., 'api' if you want node server start on docker container port other than default http://localhost:3001
# Run any commands using "RUN" command above inside your app folder. Useful for running script, setup database or build the NodeJS application before it starts in Dockerfile (optional) e .g., 'npm i' to install packages required by project , './node_modules/.bin/ng build'.
# Then use this line of code as a replacement with your actual command that will be executed.  This is where you would specify the app server and set up any environment variables if needed (optional) e .g., 'npm start' for setting up node-server, './startup.sh'). If there are no commands specified then nothing to run inside Dockerfile
EXPOSE $PORT    #This will expose a port where our NodeJS app can be reached by host machine and docker container respectively (optional) e .g., 'api'. Also it's mandatory as per requirement for production environments. You should replace "$PORT" with your required PORT number if needed
CMD [ "/bin/bash", "-c" ]  #This command will run in Docker image, after the server starts running then execute cmd (optional) e .g., 'npm test' to start unit tests for NodeJS app. If it’s absent nothing runs inside docker container so by default no CMD line provided or not set is assumed
